g.data-narration: Welcome to Relational Algebra operations. Today we explore unary and binary operations that form the foundation of database queries.
Relational Algebra provides mathematical operations that work on tables to produce new relations, forming SQL's theoretical foundation.
g.text: A set of mathematical operations that work on relations (tables) to produce new relations.
Developed by E.F. Codd in 1970 as the theoretical foundation for database management systems.

g.data-narration: Operations are divided into two types: Unary operations work on a single relation, while binary operations combine two relations.
The SELECT operation extracts rows satisfying a condition, acting as a horizontal filter that reduces tuples based on a predicate.
Here is a practical SELECT example: extracting employees from the IT department, filtering horizontally to show only matching rows.
The PROJECT operation extracts specific columns, acting as a vertical filter while automatically eliminating duplicate tuples.
PROJECT removes unwanted columns and eliminates duplicates. Notice how multiple IT employees become one IT entry in the result.
The RENAME operation changes relation or attribute names, essential for self-joins and avoiding ambiguity in complex queries.
UNION combines all tuples from two relations, eliminating duplicates. Both relations must be union-compatible with matching attributes.
g.text: Notation: R ∪ S - combines all tuples from both relations

g.data-narration: SET DIFFERENCE returns tuples in the first relation but not in the second. The order matters - R minus S is different from S minus R.
g.text: Notation: R − S - tuples in R but not in S

g.data-narration: INTERSECTION returns tuples that exist in BOTH relations. It is commutative and can be expressed using set difference operations.
g.text: Notation: R ∩ S - tuples in both R and S

g.data-narration: CARTESIAN PRODUCT combines every tuple from the first relation with every tuple from the second, creating all possible combinations.
g.text: Notation: R × S - combines every tuple from R with every tuple from S

g.data-narration: This table summarizes key properties: operation types, union compatibility requirements, and commutative behavior for all operations.
Complex queries combine multiple operations. Here we apply SELECT to filter, then PROJECT to extract names, solving the query step by step.
g.text: Query: Find names of IT employees with salary > 50000
Used to join tables with selection condition
Often followed by SELECT for meaningful results

g.data-narration: Let us summarize the key formulas: SELECT extracts rows, PROJECT extracts columns, and binary operations combine relations in different ways.
